title: Local Topological Quantum Order and Spectral Gap Stability for the AKLT Models on the Hexagonal and Lieb Lattices
authors: Amanda Young, Bruno Nachtergaele, Andrew Jackson
abstract: We prove that the ground state of the AKLT models on the hexagonal lattice and the Lieb lattice satisfy the local topological quantum order (LTQO) condition. This will be a consequence of proving that the finite volume ground states are indistinguishable from a unique infinite volume ground state. Concretely, we identify a sequence of increasing and absorbing finite volumes for which any finite volume ground state expectation is well approximated by the infinite volume state with error decaying at a uniform exponential rate in the distance between the support of the observable and boundary of the finite volume. As a corollary to the LTQO property, we obtain that the spectral gap above the ground state in these models is stable under general small perturbations of sufficient decay. We prove these results by a detailed analysis of the polymer representation of the ground states state derived by Kennedy, Lieb and Tasaki (1988) with the necessary modifications required for proving the strong form of ground state indistinguishability needed for LTQO.
summary: This paper provides a rigorous proof that AKLT models on hexagonal and Lieb lattices possess local topological quantum order and a stable spectral gap. By using cluster expansion techniques, the authors demonstrate that the ground state is unique in the infinite volume limit and that correlations decay exponentially. This establishes the robustness of these specific quantum spin models against local perturbations.
main_problem: The paper aims to prove the existence of Local Topological Quantum Order (LTQO) and the stability of the spectral gap for AKLT models on hexagonal and Lieb lattices under small perturbations.
main_result: The authors prove that the ground states of these models satisfy the LTQO condition and that the spectral gap is stable against sufficiently small, decaying perturbations, while also establishing the exponential decay of correlations.
method: The study utilizes a polymer representation of the ground state, cluster expansion techniques, and the Kotecky-Preiss convergence criterion to analyze the thermodynamic limit and ground state indistinguishability.
model_or_system: The models are AKLT (Affleck-Kennedy-Lieb-Tasaki) models on hexagonal and Lieb lattices, featuring frustration-free, SU(2) invariant Hamiltonians consisting of projections onto maximum total spin for nearest neighbors.
key_observables: Local observables, two-point correlation functions, and the spectral gap.
important_parameters: Lattice decoration number (m), spin values (s), and the distance between the observable support and the boundary.
main_assumptions: The stability proof assumes perturbations are sufficiently small and possess sufficient decay; the results for the square lattice require m >= 1.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the construction of subgraphs and lattice decorations; Figure 2 illustrates ground state space structure and bulk-boundary maps; Figure 3 depicts various types of polymers (loops and self-avoiding walks) used in the expansion.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the lattice geometries and Hamiltonian, defines the algebraic structure of the ground states using Weyl representation, develops a bulk-boundary map, applies cluster expansion to prove LTQO and gap stability, and concludes with an appendix of numerical path-counting data.
why_interesting: This work provides rigorous mathematical foundations for the stability of topological phases and spectral gaps in many-body systems, which is fundamental for understanding the robustness of quantum states in condensed matter and many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
